title: practical english usage - Part 20
content:
•  Says is pronounced /sez/.

used instead of proved, especially as an adjective (e.g. a proven liar).

•  Prove (regular) has an irregular past participle proven which  is sometimes •  Hang is regular when used to mean 'execute by hanging'.

•    The  American past participle of get is either got or gotten (►  472.6).

fit-fit- fit, quit - quit - quit •  Fit and quit are usually irregular in American English.

Why are you Jumping up and down?

suggests repetition.

When  a progressive is used to refer to a short momentary action, it often (temporary) We'll have to phone the plumber - water's running down the kitchen wall.

- The Rhine runs into the North Sea. (permanent) I've been reading a lot of thrillers recently. (not necessarily completed) - I've read your email. (completed action) rather than ‘ progressive tenses'.) Compare: permanent. (Because of this, grammars often talk about 'progressive aspect' speaker sees the event - generally as ongoing and temporary, not completed or A  progressive does not simply show the time of an event. It also shows how  the 2 terminology         and   use I didn't know how long she had been sitting there. (past perfect progressive) Progressive and perfect forms can be combined.

I'd like to be lying on the beach now. (progressive infinitive) Will you be going out this evening? (future progressive) She phoned while I was cooking. (past progressive) Your suit is being cleaned. (present progressive passive) I am waiting for the shops to open. (present progressive) Progressive verb structures (also called 'continuous') are made with be + -ing.
